The difference is that they learned how it is done, and the community remembers how to hold them. Whoever crossed a whole fissure is not made immune to the next ones; they are left, rather, with less terror of them, because they already know, from within, that the fall of a form can be the beginning of a larger one.

There is a reason for beginning this Part of the book here, at the intimate instant when a form ceases to organise. It is that the fissure of a person and the fissure of a culture have the same structure. A form made for a certain regime meets a complexity it can no longer contain, gives way, opens a void, disorients, and asks for a larger form. The scale changes; the crossing does not. What in a person is lived as an identity ringing hollow, in a culture is lived as a shared narrative about what a good life is that begins to ring false to many at once, as roles and institutions that keep functioning by inertia and no longer organise anyone. What we call human nature may be, in good part, the form that millennia of scarcity carved, and which now saturates before a real that no longer fits within it. If it is so, what so many people cross today, that misfit which has no solution within the old form because what asks to change is the form itself, is not merely private unease.

It is the version cut to the measure of whoever lives it of a much larger fissure. A culture can treat this moment in two ways, and the choice between them is the central lesson of all that follows: it can read it as a threat and run to restore the form that crumbled, tightening it, prolonging the suffering and aborting the emergence; or it can recognise it as a collective threshold and build the means, the communities, the languages, the rites, that hold the one who crosses while the new form is not yet born.

The first thing this asks is that one learn to distinguish, in the dark and without the comfort of hindsight, the disorientation that generates from the one that only destroys. Before knowing where one is going, one must find out how to cross a terrain that has no map yet. What continents are these, uncharted, that open when the old world ceases to organise the journey?
unidades.ideia: What we call crisis is often the noise of a reorganisation: a form made for a certain regime of life meets a real that no longer fits within it and gives way. The collapse is not a fault to be mended but a symbolic death that asks for mourning, and the void that opens is not only loss, it is the interval where a larger form gestates.
unidades.pergunta: What continents are these, uncharted, that open when the old world ceases to organise the journey?
destaque: But there is a decisive difference between two voids that look alike from outside.
